1: ‘When the rule of Rehoboam was established (wĕḵikôn malkût rĕḥaḇʿām) and he was strong (ûḵĕḥazzĕqātô), he abandoned (ʿāzaḇ) the law of YHWH.’ The Chronicler’s prosperity-apostasy pattern (cf. 2 Chr 17:6/12 → 17:19; 26:5 → 26:16): strength leads to self-reliance which leads to forsaking YHWH. ʿāzaḇ (to forsake, abandon) is the apostasy verb par excellence — the same word used of Israel forsaking YHWH (Judg 2:12; 2 Chr 15:2; 24:20). ‘And all Israel with him’ (wĕḵol-yiśrāʾēl ʿimmô) — the king’s apostasy implicates the whole community.
2: In the fifth year of King Rehoboam (baššānāh haḥămîšît), Shishak king of Egypt (šîšaq melek-miṣrayim) came up against Jerusalem. Historical synchronism: Pharaoh Sheshonq I (c. 945–924 BC) left an inscription at the temple of Karnak listing 150+ Palestinian cities he captured; the list includes sites in both Judah and Israel. Shishak’s campaign is the first historically confirmable event of the divided monarchy period. ‘Because they had been unfaithful to YHWH’ (ʾăšer māʿălû bĕ-YHWH) — māʿal (to act treacherously, commit sacrilege against) is the Chronicler’s guilt-term for covenant violation.
3: Shishak came with 1,200 chariots, 60,000 horsemen, and innumerable troops: Libyans (lûḇîm), Sukkites (sukkiyyîm), and Cushites (kûšîm). sukkiyyîm appears only here in the OT — a hapax legomenon probably referring to Libyan auxiliary troops or a North African tribal group. lûḇîm (Libyans) and kûšîm (Cushites/Nubians) represent Egypt’s multinational empire. The coalition’s diversity emphasizes Egypt’s imperial reach — and the magnitude of the threat against which Judah has no human defense.
4: He took (wayyilkōḏ) the fortified cities (ʿārê hammaṣṣûrôt) of Judah and came to Jerusalem. lākaḏ (to capture, seize) is the capture-of-cities vocabulary throughout Joshua and the Deuteronomistic history. The irony is precise: the very cities that Rehoboam had just fortified (ch11:5–12) fall to Shishak in his fifth year. The fortified defensive network built for security cannot withstand YHWH’s judgment.
5: Shemaiah the prophet came to Rehoboam and the leaders of Judah who had gathered at Jerusalem. ‘YHWH says: You have abandoned me (ʿăzaḇtem ʾōtî), so I have abandoned you to the hand of Shishak (wĕgam-ʾănî ʿāzaḇtî ʾetḵem bĕyaḏ-šîšāq).’ The talion structure is explicit: the same verb (ʿāzaḇ) is used for the people’s action and YHWH’s response. Forsaking produces forsaking. The divine lex talionis is a consistent pattern in the Chronicler’s retributive theology.
6: ‘The leaders of Israel and the king humbled themselves (wayyikkānĕʿû śārê yiśrāʾēl wĕhammelek) and said: YHWH is righteous (ṣaddîq YHWH).’ kānaʿ (Niphal: to humble oneself, be subdued) is the Chronicler’s signature repentance verb, appearing in the famous conditional promise of 2 Chr 7:14 (‘if my people... humble themselves’). Acknowledging ‘YHWH is righteous’ (ṣaddîq YHWH) means accepting that the divine judgment is just, not arbitrary. The confession of divine righteousness in the face of punishment is the OT pattern of genuine repentance (Neh 9:33; Dan 9:14).
7: ‘When YHWH saw (wayyarʾ YHWH) that they humbled themselves, the word of YHWH came to Shemaiah: They have humbled themselves (niknaʿû); I will not destroy them (lōʾ ʾašḥîtēm), but I will grant them some deliverance (wĕnātattî lāhem kĕmĕʿaṭ lĕpālēṭāh).’ šāḥat (Hiphil: to destroy, ruin) — the most severe punishment is averted. kĕmĕʿaṭ = a little, some (diminished amount); pālēṭāh = escape, deliverance. The partial deliverance formula signals the Chronicler’s graduated response theology: full repentance yields full restoration; partial humbling yields partial relief.
8: ‘But they will be his servants (wĕhāyû-lô laʿăḇādîm) so that they may know the difference between serving me and serving the kingdoms of the countries.’ The purpose clause is theological pedagogy: subjection to Shishak will teach what they failed to learn in freedom. ʿāḇaḏ (to serve, be a slave) applied both to serving YHWH (vocation) and serving Shishak (punishment). The distinction is: free people serve YHWH voluntarily; enslaved people serve foreign kings by compulsion. Sin converts vocation into servitude.
9: Shishak king of Egypt came up against Jerusalem and took (wayyiqqaḥ) the treasuries (ʾōṣĕrôt) of the house of YHWH and the treasuries of the king’s house. He took everything, including the golden shields (māginnê hazzāhāḇ) that Solomon had made. The golden shields of 9:15-16, symbols of Solomonic peak glory, are now stripped away. The treasury-taking is a reversal: what the nations brought to Jerusalem under Solomon (9:24) now flows back out to the nations under Rehoboam.
10: King Rehoboam made in their place bronze shields (māginnê nĕḥōšet) and gave them in charge of the officers of the guard (śārê hārāṣîm). Bronze replaces gold: a visible, material demotion. The guard officers who carried the gold shields under Solomon now carry bronze substitutes. The degradation is architectural and economic: the temple precincts display a diminished glory corresponding to the diminished spiritual condition.
11: As often as the king entered the house of YHWH, the guard came and carried them, then brought them back to the guardroom (liškat hārāṣîm). The ceremonial function of the shields is preserved despite their material demotion. The ritual continues — the form without the substance — which may be the Chronicler’s implicit critique: the appearance of worship maintained even after the gold of devotion has been stripped away.
12: ‘Because he humbled himself (šĕhiḵkānîʿa), the wrath of YHWH (ʾap YHWH) turned away (šāḇ) from him and did not destroy him completely.’ ʾap (wrath, nostril) = YHWH’s anger expressed in physical-emotional terms. šûḇ (to turn, return) applied to divine wrath: the turning-away of YHWH’s face/anger is the reversal of the prophetic judgment. ‘Indeed, there were good things in Judah (ḵî gam-bîhûḏāh hāyû dĕḇārîm ṭôḇîm).’ ṭôḇ (good) as the narrator’s summary verdict: partial faithfulness yields a partial positive assessment.
13: King Rehoboam was strong (wayyitḥazzēq) in Jerusalem and reigned. He was 41 years old when he began to reign (ben-ʾarbaʿîm wĕʾaḥat šānāh rĕḥaḇʿām bĕmolĕkô) and reigned 17 years in Jerusalem. His mother was Naamah (naʿămāh) the Ammonitess. naʿămāh = pleasant, lovely (the same name as Naaman the Aramean general in 2 Kgs 5). Naamah’s Ammonite origin is deliberately noted: Solomon’s Deuteronomy-forbidden foreign wives (1 Kgs 11:1) have dynastic consequences — the divided kingdom is, in part, their theological legacy.
14: ‘He did evil, because he did not set his heart (lōʾ hēkîn lĕḇāḇô) to seek YHWH.’ kûn (Hiphil: to establish, prepare, make firm) applied to the heart: hēkîn lēḇ = to prepare/establish the heart, the idiom for wholehearted devotion. David used this idiom positively (1 Chr 22:19: ‘set your heart and soul to seek YHWH your God’); Rehoboam fails it negatively. The ‘prepared heart’ is the Chronicler’s standard for Davidic faithfulness; its absence defines the failure of Rehoboam’s reign.
15: The acts of Rehoboam, from first to last (hāriʾšōnîm wĕhāʾaḥărōnîm), are written in the records of Shemaiah the prophet and of Iddo the seer (yĕʿaddô ḥōzeh). ḥōzeh (seer) vs. nāḇîʾ (prophet): Shemaiah is called nāḇîʾ (v5) and Iddo is called ḥōzeh — the two prophetic modes. The citation of dual prophetic sources provides double witness for the regnal record. mityaḥēś (genealogical registration, Hithpael of yāḥas) also appears: there were wars between Rehoboam and Jeroboam throughout their reigns.
16: ‘Rehoboam slept with his fathers (wayyiškab rĕḥaḇʿām ʿim-ʾăḇōtāyw) and was buried in the city of David. Abijah his son reigned in his place.’ The standard regnal death formula. Burial in ‘the city of David’ maintains Rehoboam within the Davidic dynasty’s sacred geography despite his faithlessness. The succession to Abijah — the son designated as nāgîḏ in 11:22 — confirms that Rehoboam’s earlier dynastic planning succeeded even if his spiritual legacy failed.
